steps.title: Test the concern
steps.content: The initial concern was that free virtual tours would make an in-person visit to the museum seem unnecessary. To support the administrators, the survey evidence must show that virtual tours did not discourage in-person visits and may even have encouraged them. Evidence about visitors' location or later online viewing would miss that cause-and-effect issue about planning a visit.

steps.title: Eliminate irrelevant surveys
steps.content:
• Choice B says visitors were unaware of the virtual tours, so the tours could not have affected their decision.
  • Choice C says where visitors lived, not why they visited.
  • Choice D concerns viewing virtual tours after visiting, which does not address the original concern.

steps.title: Show tours encouraged visits
steps.content: Choice A says many surveyed visitors indicated that the virtual tours convinced them to plan an in-person visit. That is the opposite of making a visit seem unnecessary, so it most directly supports the administrators' claim about the concern being unjustified. The answer is A.
